findings.title: Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider
findings.summary: Summary The Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) is releasing this advisory in response to ransomware actors leveraging unpatched instances of a vulnerability in SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) to compromise customers of a utility billing software provider. This incident reflects a broader pattern of ransomware actors targeting organizations through unpatched versions of SimpleHelp RMM since January 2025. SimpleHelp versions 5.5.7 and earlier contain several vulnerabilities, including CVE-2024-57727 —a path traversal vulnerability. 1 Ransomware actors likely leveraged CVE-2024-57727 to access downstream customers’ unpatched SimpleHelp RMM for disruption of services in double extortion compromises. 1 CISA added CVE-2024-57727 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) Catalog on Feb. 13, 2025. CISA urges software vendors, downstream customers, and end users to immediately implement the Mitigations listed in this advisory based on confirmed compromise or risk of compromise. Download the PDF version of this report: AA25-163A Ransomware Actors Exploit Unpatched SimpleHelp Remote Monitoring and Management to Compromise Utility Billing Software Provider (PDF, 420.49 KB ) Mitigations CISA recommends organizations implement the mitigations below to respond to emerging ransomware activity exploiting SimpleHelp software. These mitigations align with the Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals (CPGs) developed by CISA and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST). The CPGs provide a minimum set of practices and protections that CISA and NIST recommend all organizations implement. CISA and NIST based the CPGs on existing cybersecurity frameworks and guidance to protect against the most common and impactful threats, tactics, techniques, and procedures. Visit CISA’s CPGs webpage for more information on the CPGs, including additional recommended baseline protections. These mitigations apply to all critical infrastructure

enrichment.technical_details: CWE-22 path traversal in the SimpleHelp web application: the `filepath` request parameter is not properly sanitized, allowing an unauthenticated remote attacker to use `../` sequences to escape the intended directory and read arbitrary files on the SimpleHelp host. Exposed data includes SimpleHelp server configuration with secrets (e.g., licensing tokens) and hashed Technician/Administrator passwords, plus general host files such as /etc/passwd. Disclosed by Horizon3.ai on 2025-01-13 alongside CVE-2024-57726 and CVE-2024-57728.
enrichment.affected_products: SimpleHelp Remote Support / Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) software, version 5.5.7 and all earlier releases (covers the 5.5.x, 5.4.x, and 5.3.x mainlines; patched in v5.5.8, v5.4.10, and v5.3.9).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public PoC on GitHub (https://github.com/imjdl/CVE-2024-57727, `python poc.py <http://target>`); OffSec reports a Metasploit auxiliary module (auxiliary/scanner/http/simplehelp_toolbox_path_traversal) and a Rapid7 Exploit Database entry.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — SimpleHelp shipped v5.5.8 (and subsequent 5.5.x updates), v5.4.10, and v5.3.9 within ~2 days of the January 13, 2025 disclosure. Patches and upgrade instructions: https://simple-help.com/kb---security-vulnerabilities-01-2025 (mirrored at guides.simple-help.com).
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. CVE-2024-57727 was added to the CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog on 2025-02-13. CISA advisory AA25-163A (2025-06-12) reports DragonForce ransomware actors exploited unpatched SimpleHelp RMM (including CVE-2024-57727) at a utility billing software provider since January 2025, using the access for double-extortion attacks against downstream customers. Corroborated by ThreatDown (2025-06-18) and Halcyon (2025-06-24).
enrichment.threat_actors: DragonForce ransomware actors. CISA advisory AA25-163A attributes exploitation of unpatched SimpleHelp RMM (including CVE-2024-57727) — targeting a utility billing software provider's downstream customers — to DragonForce since January 2025; corroborated by ThreatDown and Halcyon reporting.
enrichment.mitigation: Upgrade to SimpleHelp v5.5.8 or later (full installer). For v5.4.x branch: replace lib/shelp-jar-with-dependencies.jar with the 5.4.10 patched jar after stopping the service. For v5.3.x branch: replace lib/secure_utils.jar, lib/secure_nlink.jar, and lib/secure_shelp.jar with the 5.3.9 patched jars after stopping the service. Additional hardening: change the SimpleHelp Administrator password; change local (non-SSO/AD) Technician account passwords; restrict source IPs allowed to log in as Technician or Administrator. If immediate patching is impossible, isolate the SimpleHelp server from the internet or stop the server process.

findings.title: Unpatched Flaws Disclosed in Filesystem Bundled Into Millions of Embedded Devices
findings.summary:
Security firm runZero has disclosed seven vulnerabilities in FatFs, a small filesystem library that lets a device read and write the FAT and exFAT formats used on USB drives and SD cards.

The flaws matter because FatFs is nearly everywhere. It ships inside the firmware that runs security cameras, drones, industrial controllers, hardware crypto wallets, and other devices built on

enrichment.technical_details: Seven flaws in ChaN FatFs R0.16 and earlier, all triggered by a crafted FAT/exFAT/GPT image delivered via removable media (USB/SD) or OTA channels. (1) CVE-2026-6682 — integer overflow in FAT32 mount_volume (fasize *= fs->n_fats wraps), CWE-190; memory corruption/code execution. (2) CVE-2026-6683 — divide-by-zero in exFAT sync/write when n_fatent-2 == 0, CWE-369; device crash / OTA bricking. (3) CVE-2026-6684 — unbounded GPT partition-scan loop (GPTH_PtNum trusted without cap), CWE-835; DoS/hang. (4) CVE-2026-6685 — unsigned-subtraction wrap in dirty-cache handling (fp->sect - sect < cc), CWE-191; stale-cache OOB, silent data corruption. (5) CVE-2026-6686 — uninitialized clusters on f_lseek beyond EOF, CWE-908; data leakage. (6) CVE-2026-6687 — stack-based buffer overflow in f_getlabel (XDIR_NumLabel unbounded), CWE-121; arbitrary code execution. (7) CVE-2026-6688 — long-filename overflow in downstream wrappers copying fno.fname (up to 255 chars) into fixed-size buffers, CWE-120; buffer overflow in caller code. All attack vectors are Physical (AV:P).
enrichment.affected_products: ChaN FatFs R0.16 and earlier (R0.13 through R0.16b), shipped in firmware using: Espressif ESP-IDF, STMicroelectronics STM32Cube middleware, Zephyr RTOS, MicroPython, ArduPilot, RT-Thread, Arm Mbed, Samsung TizenRT, SWUpdate, Rene Stange's Circle, Adafruit TinyUF2, and Eugene Tarassov's Vivado RISC-V build. Affected device classes: security cameras, drones, industrial controllers, hardware crypto wallets, and other embedded/IoT devices mounting USB drives or SD cards.
enrichment.cvss_vector: Per-CVE CVSS v3.1 vectors: CVE-2026-6682 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (7.6); CVE-2026-6683 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H (4.6); CVE-2026-6684 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:N/A:H (4.6); CVE-2026-6685 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:N/I:H/A:H (6.1); CVE-2026-6686 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:N/A:N (4.6); CVE-2026-6687 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (7.6); CVE-2026-6688 = AV:P/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H (7.6).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public proof-of-concept available. runZero published exploit bundle including corrupt FatFs disk images, a test harness, and a working QEMU-based exploit demonstrating memory-corruption reach on the high-rated bugs (CVE-2026-6682, -6687, -6688). Source: https://github.com/runZeroInc/vulns-2026-fatfs-chance
enrichment.patch_available: Partial — only CVE-2026-6684 (GPT partition-scan loop DoS) is fixed upstream in ChaN's FatFs R0.16 (September 13, 2025 with later patch). The other six CVEs (CVE-2026-6682, -6683, -6685, -6686, -6687, -6688) have NO upstream ChaN fix as of 2026-07-03. Mitigation falls to each downstream integrator (ESP-IDF, STM32Cube, Zephyr, MicroPython, ArduPilot, RT-Thread, Mbed, TizenRT, SWUpdate, Circle, TinyUF2, Vivado RISC-V, etc.) to patch their own fork/wrapper. Upstream site: https://elm-chan.org/fsw/ff/
enrichment.active_exploitation: None known. runZero's July 1, 2026 disclosure and subsequent reporting (The Hacker News, Mallory, Quasar Pulse, July 3–4 2026) all state that no attacks using these bugs had been observed in the wild. The public PoC repository at https://github.com/runZeroInc/vulns-2026-fatfs-chance is for defensive research only.
enrichment.mitigation: Until downstream fixes ship: (a) audit bundled FatFs in your product — inspect wrapper code around f_getlabel(), f_read/f_write, f_lseek, and anywhere fno.fname or 64-bit size metadata is handled; look for fixed-size buffers and unbounded copies; plan to backport fixes; (b) treat physical media ports and OTA/update channels as an attack surface — restrict who can plug in USB/SD media, validate/scan images where possible, and monitor vendor firmware/security bulletins for ESP-IDF, STM32Cube, Zephyr, MicroPython, ArduPilot, RT-Thread, Mbed, TizenRT, SWUpdate, Circle, TinyUF2, and Vivado RISC-V; (c) avoid processing untrusted FAT/exFAT images on devices running FatFs R0.16 or earlier; (d) for the GPT DoS specifically, upgrade bundled FatFs to R0.16 (the only one of seven with an upstream ChaN fix); (e) data-leakage risk (CVE-2026-6686) on multi-tenant media — assess and apply overwrite-on-extend where feasible.

findings.title: AI threats in the wild: The current state of prompt injections on the web
findings.summary: Posted by Thomas Brunner, Yu-Han Liu, Moni Pande At Google, our Threat Intelligence teams are dedicated to staying ahead of real-world adversarial activity, proactively monitoring emerging threats before they can impact users. Right now, Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a top priority for the security community, anticipating it as a primary attack vector for adversaries to target and compromise AI agents. But while the danger of IPI is widely discussed, are threat actors actually exploiting this vector today – and if so, how? To answer these questions and to uncover real-world abuse, we initiated a broad sweep of the public web to monitor for known indirect prompt injection patterns. This is what we found. The threat of indirect prompt injection Unlike a direct injection where a user "jailbreaks" a chatbot, IPI occurs when an AI system processes content—like a website, email, or document—that contains malicious instructions. When the AI reads this poisoned content, it may silently follow the attacker's commands instead of the user's original intent. This is not a new area of concern for us and Google has been working tirelessly to combat these threats. Our efforts involve cross-functional collaboration between researchers at Google DeepMind (GDM) and defenders like the Google Threat Intelligence Group (GTIG). We have previously detailed our work in this area and researchers have further highlighted the evolving nature of these vulnerabilities. Despite this collective focus, a fundamental question remains: to what degree are real-world malicious actors currently operationalizing these attacks? Proactive monitoring at Google The landscape of IPI on the web There are many channels through which attackers might try to send prompt injections. However, one location is particularly easy to observe - the public web. Here, threat actors may simply seed prompt injections on websites in hope of corrupting AI systems that browse them. Public research confirms these attacks ar

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is an attack where adversaries never address the AI directly but instead plant adversarial instructions inside content an AI agent will later ingest — public web pages, HTML comments, hidden CSS layers, metadata, or shared documents connected to a RAG corpus. Observed mechanisms include: (a) role impersonation with conditional targeting ('If you are an AI assistant...'); (b) system-override tag spoofing; (c) CSS concealment (visibility:hidden, display:none, 1px transparent text); (d) exfiltration via external image/URL parameters; (e) data-destruction prompts; (f) AI denial-of-service via infinite-text/timeout waste. GeminiJack exploited the trust boundary between user-controlled Workspace content and Gemini Enterprise's RAG pipeline, exfiltrating Gmail, Calendar, and Docs data via an attacker-controlled image URL.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Gemini (consumer/Enterprise); Google Workspace with Gemini (Gmail, Docs, Calendar); Google Gemini Enterprise; Google Vertex AI Search; AI agents integrated into web browsers and search engines; GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and Claude Code (per Forcepoint X-Labs telemetry). Specific build/version numbers were not published.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Multiple public PoC artifacts and weaponized payloads exist: (1) Forcepoint X-Labs documented 10 in-the-wild IPI payloads (https://www.forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads); (2) Noma Labs published GeminiJack zero-click PoC and demo video (https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/); (3) Public GitHub PoC repository (https://github.com/brennanbrown/atlas-prompt-injection-poc); (4) Palo Alto Unit 42 documented 12 real-world IPI incidents (https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/ai-agent-prompt-injection/).
enrichment.patch_available: Partial. Google shipped a concrete fix for the GeminiJack zero-click flaw in Gemini Enterprise and Vertex AI Search by December 10, 2025, including fully separating Vertex AI Search from Gemini Enterprise. Google continues to ship ongoing defensive hardening for Workspace-with-Gemini. However, IPI as a vulnerability class cannot be eliminated by a single patch — mitigations are layered and continuous. Advisory URL: https://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability/
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active exploitation in the wild: (1) Google's Threat Intelligence Group scan of Common Crawl found IPI payloads on real, publicly reachable websites, with a 32% relative increase in the malicious category between November 2025 and February 2026; (2) Forcepoint X-Labs documented 10 live IPI payloads on April 22, 2026; (3) Palo Alto Unit 42 documented 12 real-world IPI incidents on March 3, 2026; (4) Noma Labs GeminiJack PoC demonstrated zero-click exfiltration against live Gemini Enterprise instances.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. The Google post and supporting research describe actors generically as 'threat actors,' 'adversaries,' and 'individual website authors' (including hostile SEO operators and AI-deterring site owners). No named APT groups or ransomware operators are attributed to the observed IPI activity.
enrichment.mitigation: Google's layered defense: (1) Deterministic defenses — user-confirmation prompts, URL/link sanitization, tool-chaining allowlists; (2) ML-based defenses — retraining on synthetic IPI data; (3) LLM-based defenses — refined system-prompt engineering and instruction-hierarchy enforcement; (4) model hardening; (5) continuous human + automated red-teaming; (6) Google AI Vulnerability Reward Program. Palo Alto Unit 42 recommends 'spotlighting' (separating untrusted text using delimiters/markers), instruction hierarchy, adversarial training, and web-scale intent/visibility analysis products. Forcepoint X-Labs recommends blocklisting known IPI URLs and enforcing a strict data-vs-instruction boundary.

findings.title: Google Workspace’s continuous approach to mitigating indirect prompt injections
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team Indirect prompt injection (IPI) is an evolving threat vector targeting users of complex AI applications with multiple data sources, such as Workspace with Gemini. This technique enables the attacker to influence the behavior of an LLM by injecting malicious instructions into the data or tools used by the LLM as it completes the user’s query. This may even be possible without any input directly from the user. IPI is not the kind of technical problem you “solve” and move on. Sophisticated LLMs with increasing use of agentic automation combined with a wide range of content create an ultra-dynamic and evolving playground for adversarial attacks. That’s why Google takes a sophisticated and comprehensive approach to these attacks. We’re continuously improving LLM resistance to IPI attacks and launching AI application capabilities with ever-improving defenses. Staying ahead of the latest indirect prompt injection attacks is critical to our mission of securing Workspace with Gemini. In our previous blog “ Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy ”, we reviewed the layered architecture of our IPI defenses. In this blog, we’ll share more detail on the continuous approach we take to improve these defenses and to solve for new attacks. New attack discovery By proactively discovering and cataloging new attack vectors through internal and external programs, we can identify vulnerabilities and deploy robust defenses ahead of adversarial activity. Human Red-Teaming Human Red-Teaming uses adversarial simulations to uncover security and safety vulnerabilities. Specialized teams execute attacks based on realistic user profiles to exploit weaknesses, coordinating with product teams to resolve identified issues. Automated Red-Teaming Automated Red-Teaming is done via dynamic, machine-learning-driven frameworks to stress-test environments. By algorithmically generating and iterating on attack payloads, we can mi

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect Prompt Injection (IPI) is a class of attack in which an adversary embeds malicious natural-language (or hidden) instructions inside external content that an LLM later consumes—webpages scraped by an AI browser/summarizer, documents ingested by a RAG pipeline, or emails fetched through tool-use/function-calling. When the model retrieves or summarizes that content, the injected instructions override or subvert the user's original prompt, causing the agent to call tools or generate output the user did not request. Specific techniques include: payload splitting across documents (e.g., a resume), multimodal injection hidden in images, obfuscation (Base64, emoji, multi-language), adversarial suffixes, hidden text (white-on-white, visually-hidden CSS classes), code injection in function-calling outputs, and cross-document exfiltration where one document instructs Gemini to forward data from another. End-to-end chain Google documented: malicious trigger (e.g., 'Linear Generation' procedural text) embedded in email/document → Gemini retrieves data via tool use → trigger forces a function call that exfiltrates private user data (passwords, SSNs, passport numbers) to attacker-controlled endpoint. No direct user input required beyond the normal query that pulls untrusted content.
enrichment.affected_products: Google Workspace with Gemini (Gemini integrated in Gmail, Google Docs editors, Google Drive, and Google Chat); standalone Gemini app; Gemini model family (Gemini 1.5, Gemini 2.0, Gemini 2.5). Broader class: any agentic LLM application that ingests untrusted external content (web pages, files, emails) including RAG pipelines, AI coding assistants, and AI browsing/summarization agents.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — multiple public PoCs and in-the-wild payloads. Examples: HiddenLayer 'New Gemini for Workspace Vulnerability' (https://www.hiddenlayer.com/research/new-gemini-for-workspace-vulnerability-enabling-phishing-content-manipulation); 0din.ai 'Phishing For Gemini' (https://0din.ai/blog/phishing-for-gemini); Forcepoint X-Labs '10 Indirect Prompt Injection Payloads Caught in the Wild' (https://www.forcepoint.com/blog/x-labs/indirect-prompt-injection-payloads, Apr 22 2026); Praetorian 'Indirect Prompt Injection: The LLM Supervisor Blind Spot' (https://www.praetorian.com/blog/indirect-prompt-injection-llm/); Google's arXiv 2505.14534 demonstrating end-to-end IPI against RAG and agentic systems.
enrichment.patch_available: No single discrete patch version. The April 2, 2026 Google blog explicitly frames IPI mitigation as a continuous, evolving defense program with model and policy updates shipped continuously rather than as a CVE-numbered fix. Related Google patch-equivalent updates: (a) URL-based exfiltration mitigations in Gemini (https://bughunters.google.com/blog/mitigating-url-based-exfiltration-in-gemini, Mar 9, 2026); (b) Gemini protected-material / function-calling hardening described in arXiv 2505.14534. Workspace admins should monitor https://workspaceupdates.google.com for ongoing Gemini feature/security updates. No public CVE identifier has been assigned to the IPI class in Workspace with Gemini (EchoLeak CVE-2025-32711 covers Microsoft 365 Copilot; CVE-2026-30741 covers OpenClaw Agent Platform).
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes—confirmed active exploitation in the wild. Forcepoint X-Labs publicly reported on April 22, 2026 that they observed 10 distinct IPI payloads on live public websites spanning: API-key exfiltration (thelibrary-welcome[.]uk), content suppression / DoS (fake copyright notices on bentasker[.]co[.]uk), traffic hijacking (luminousmen[.]com, kassoon[.]com), data destruction (sudo rm payload on faladobairro[.]com), financial fraud (PayPal.me $5,000 link on perceptivepumpkin[.]com), Stripe donation scams via 'ai:action' namespace injection (archibase[.]co), and canary probes abusing accessibility attributes (lawsofux[.]com). HelpNetSecurity corroborated this on April 24, 2026 (https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2026/04/24/indirect-prompt-injection-in-the-wild/). SecurityWeek reported (April 2026) that Google's own broad sweep of the public web found increasing IPI activity. Independent researcher reports (HiddenLayer 2024, 0din.ai Jul 2025) demonstrated successful exploitation prior to these in-the-wild observations.
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. No specific APT group, cybercriminal gang, or ransomware operator has been publicly attributed to exploiting indirect prompt injection (IPI) against Workspace with Gemini. Public reporting refers generically to attackers embedding instructions in untrusted content (web pages, emails, documents) and describes payload intents (API-key exfiltration, financial fraud, content suppression) but does not name specific threat actors.
enrichment.mitigation: Google employs a continuous, layered defense strategy: (1) Deterministic defenses—centralized Policy Engine enforcing user confirmation, URL/markdown sanitization, suspicious-URL redaction, and tool-chaining policies; (2) ML-based defenses—classifier models trained on synthetic IPI data expanded via the 'Simula' framework; (3) LLM-based defenses—refined system instructions (security thought reinforcement) pushing the model to ignore embedded directives; (4) Model resilience—retraining Gemini to internally identify and ignore harmful instructions in data, adversarial fine-tuning, in-context learning with positive/negative IPI examples, Retrieved-Data Classifier and User-Instruction Classifier. Recommended hardening for users/admins: ensure user confirmation prompts for risky operations are enabled, review end-user security-mitigation notifications surfaced by Gemini, restrict Gemini extensions/tools to a least-privilege set, separate untrusted external content via content-class boundaries, require human-in-the-loop approval for high-risk actions. OWASP LLM01:2025 guidance: least-privilege tool design (handle sensitive functions in code rather than via the model), output-format validation, semantic filtering, the RAG Triad (context relevance, groundedness, Q&A relevance), and regular adversarial testing.

findings.title: Architecting Security for Agentic Capabilities in Chrome
findings.summary: Posted by Nathan Parker, Chrome security team Chrome has been advancing the web’s security for well over 15 years, and we’re committed to meeting new challenges and opportunities with AI. Billions of people trust Chrome to keep them safe by default, and this is a responsibility we take seriously. Following the recent launch of Gemini in Chrome and the preview of agentic capabilities , we want to share our approach and some new innovations to improve the safety of agentic browsing. The primary new threat facing all agentic browsers is indirect prompt injection . It can appear in malicious sites, third-party content in iframes, or from user-generated content like user reviews, and can cause the agent to take unwanted actions such as initiating financial transactions or exfiltrating sensitive data. Given this open challenge, we are investing in a layered defense that includes both deterministic and probabilistic defenses to make it difficult and costly for attackers to cause harm. Designing safe agentic browsing for Chrome has involved deep collaboration of security experts across Google. We built on Gemini's existing protections and agent security principles and have implemented several new layers for Chrome. We’re introducing a user alignment critic where the agent’s actions are vetted by a separate model that is isolated from untrusted content. We’re also extending Chrome’s origin-isolation capabilities to constrain what origins the agent can interact with, to just those that are relevant to the task. Our layered defense also includes user confirmations for critical steps, real-time detection of threats , and red-teaming and response . We’ll step through these layers below. Checking agent outputs with User Alignment Critic The main planning model for Gemini uses page content shared in Chrome to decide what action to take next. Exposure to untrusted web content means it is inherently vulnerable to indirect prompt injection. We use techniques like spotlighting that di

enrichment.technical_details: The primary technical threat is indirect prompt injection (IPI): an attacker embeds hidden or disguised instructions inside otherwise-normal web content (e.g., a malicious site, iframe, calendar invite, shared document, or email) that the Gemini-in-Chrome agent ingests while performing a user-authorized task. Because the LLM cannot reliably distinguish user/system instructions from page content, embedded instructions can cause harmful actions (financial transactions, data exfiltration, credential theft, malware installation). The related CVE-2026-0628 follows a different but adjacent vector: insufficient policy enforcement in Chrome's <webview> tag and declarativeNetRequests API allowed a malicious Chrome extension to inject JavaScript/HTML into Chrome's privileged Gemini panel, granting access to local files, camera, microphone, and arbitrary site screenshots (CWE-862 Missing Authorization).
enrichment.affected_products: Google Chrome with Gemini in Chrome agentic capabilities (general class described in the advisory). The closely related public CVE tied to Chrome's Gemini panel insufficient policy enforcement is CVE-2026-0628 in Google Chrome prior to version 143.0.7499.192 (CWE-862: Missing Authorization).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. Public proof-of-concept code is available. A PoC for CVE-2026-0628 demonstrating script injection into privileged Chrome pages (including the Gemini panel) via the <webview> tag in a malicious Chrome extension is at https://github.com/fevar54/CVE-2026-0628-POC. Additional PoC-style research demonstrations include Noma Labs' GeminiJack (http://noma.security/blog/geminijack-google-gemini-zero-click-vulnerability) and Palo Alto Unit 42 (http://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/gemini-live-in-chrome-hijacking).
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. The Chrome stable channel update for desktop published on January 6, 2026 shipped Chrome 143.0.7499.192 and patched CVE-2026-0628 insufficient policy enforcement in the WebView tag. Release notes: https://chromereleases.googleblog.com/2026/01/stable-channel-update-for-desktop.html. The agentic security architecture described in the December 8, 2025 blog is delivered continuously via Chrome's normal auto-update channel.
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation directly tied to this specific advisory or CVE-2026-0628 at the time of publication. SecurityWeek (December 8, 2025) quoted Google: 'While no in-the-wild exploitation of these vulnerabilities has been observed.' The NVD/OffSeq Threat Radar for CVE-2026-0628 likewise reports no known exploits in the wild. Demonstrations were coordinated, responsibly disclosed research (Noma Labs GeminiJack, Palo Alto Unit 42 Chrome hijacking, Miggo calendar invite attack). Broader indirect-prompt-injection activity has since been documented as emerging in the wild generally (HelpNet Security, April 24, 2026: 'Indirect prompt injection is taking hold in the wild'; Unit 42, March 3, 2026: 'Web-Based Indirect Prompt Injection Observed in the Wild').
enrichment.threat_actors: None known. Google's December 8, 2025 announcement and SecurityWeek's coverage do not name any specific threat actor, APT group, or ransomware campaign exploiting these vulnerabilities. SecurityWeek explicitly states: 'While no in-the-wild exploitation of these vulnerabilities has been observed.'
enrichment.mitigation: Chrome's new agentic security architecture applies several layered defenses: (1) a User Alignment Critic—a separate Gemini-based high-trust model reviewing agent actions before execution; (2) Agent Origin Sets—restricting agent access to task-relevant origins (read-only for content, read-writable only for actuation); (3) deterministic and model-based User Confirmations for sensitive sites (e.g., Google Password Manager sign-ins) and consequential actions (e.g., payments); (4) a Real-time prompt-injection classifier running in parallel with the planning model; (5) Spotlighting—directing the model to strongly prefer following user/system instructions over page content; (6) deterministic URL checks restricting model-generated URLs to known public URLs; (7) a trustworthy gating function; (8) updated Chrome Vulnerability Rewards Program guidelines. Users should keep Chrome auto-updated and exercise caution with Gemini agentic features on untrusted sites.

findings.title: Rust in Android: move fast and fix things
findings.summary: Posted by Jeff Vander Stoep, Android Last year, we wrote about why a memory safety strategy that focuses on vulnerability prevention in new code quickly yields durable and compounding gains. This year we look at how this approach isn’t just fixing things, but helping us move faster . The 2025 data continues to validate the approach, with memory safety vulnerabilities falling below 20% of total vulnerabilities for the first time. Updated data for 2025. This data covers first-party and third-party (open source) code changes to the Android platform across C, C++, Java, Kotlin, and Rust. This post is published a couple of months before the end of 2025, but Android’s industry-standard 90-day patch window means that these results are very likely close to final. We can and will accelerate patching when necessary. We adopted Rust for its security and are seeing a 1000x reduction in memory safety vulnerability density compared to Android’s C and C++ code . But the biggest surprise was Rust's impact on software delivery. With Rust changes having a 4x lower rollback rate and spending 25% less time in code review , the safer path is now also the faster one. In this post, we dig into the data behind this shift and also cover: How we’re expanding our reach: We're pushing to make secure code the default across our entire software stack. We have updates on Rust adoption in first-party apps, the Linux kernel, and firmware. Our first rust memory safety vulnerability...almost: We'll analyze a near-miss memory safety bug in unsafe Rust: how it happened, how it was mitigated, and steps we're taking to prevent recurrence. It’s also a good chance to answer the question “if Rust can have memory safety issues, why bother at all?” Building Better Software, Faster Developing an operating system requires the low-level control and predictability of systems programming languages like C, C++, and Rust. While Java and Kotlin are important for Android platform development, their role is complementa

enrichment.technical_details: CVE-2025-48530 is an out-of-bounds (OOB) memory access vulnerability in the CrabbyAVIF Rust-based AVIF image parser/decoder in Android System. In multiple locations within CrabbyAVIF (including YUV planes, alpha plane, Y plane, UV planes, chroma width calculation, plane size calculation, and row bytes), incorrect bounds checking leads to OOB memory accesses. This could lead to remote code execution (RCE) when combined with other bugs, with no additional execution privileges required and no user interaction needed. The vulnerability is particularly notable because it occurred in 'unsafe' Rust code, demonstrating that Rust's memory safety guarantees require careful handling of unsafe blocks.
enrichment.affected_products: Android (System component - CrabbyAVIF AVIF parser/decoder), versions with security patch level before 2025-08-01
enrichment.exploit_available: No public proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit is known. Source: Feedly CVE-2025-48530 entry (https://feedly.com/cve/CVE-2025-48530) states: 'There is no evidence that a public proof-of-concept exists.'
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Patched via the Android Security Bulletin – August 2025 (security patch level 2025-08-01). Advisory URL: https://source.android.com/docs/security/bulletin/2025-08-01. The CrabbyAVIF fix commit is at https://android.googlesource.com/platform/external/rust/crabbyavif/+/42feb427a42afd9eebe6391a0d8a7b083fe82140
enrichment.active_exploitation: No confirmed active exploitation in the wild. The vulnerability was caught internally before being shipped as a public Android release, so no production code contained the flaw. Feedly's CVE-2025-48530 entry explicitly states: 'There is no evidence of proof of exploitation at the moment.' (Source: https://feedly.com/cve/CVE-2025-48530)
enrichment.mitigation: 1) Apply the Android Security Bulletin August 2025 update (security patch level 2025-08-01 or later) to address the vulnerability. 2) Android's default Scudo hardened allocator (which uses guard pages around secondary allocations) rendered this vulnerability non-exploitable by deterministically terminating the process on overflow rather than allowing silent corruption. This serves as a built-in mitigation against exploitation. 3) Google has committed to issuing CVEs for any future vulnerabilities of sufficient severity that could have been prevented by Scudo, and is pushing partners toward making Scudo mandatory on all Android devices.

findings.title: Mitigating prompt injection attacks with a layered defense strategy
findings.summary: Posted by Adam Gavish, Google GenAI Security Team With the rapid adoption of generative AI, a new wave of threats is emerging across the industry with the aim of manipulating the AI systems themselves. One such emerging attack vector is indirect prompt injections. Unlike direct prompt injections, where an attacker directly inputs malicious commands into a prompt, indirect prompt injections involve hidden malicious instructions within external data sources. These may include emails, documents, or calendar invites that instruct AI to exfiltrate user data or execute other rogue actions. As more governments, businesses, and individuals adopt generative AI to get more done, this subtle yet potentially potent attack becomes increasingly pertinent across the industry, demanding immediate attention and robust security measures. At Google, our teams have a longstanding precedent of investing in a defense-in-depth strategy, including robust evaluation, threat analysis, AI security best practices , AI red-teaming , adversarial training, and model hardening for generative AI tools. This approach enables safer adoption of Gemini in Google Workspace and the Gemini app (we refer to both in this blog as “Gemini” for simplicity). Below we describe our prompt injection mitigation product strategy based on extensive research, development, and deployment of improved security mitigations. A layered security approach Google has taken a layered security approach introducing security measures designed for each stage of the prompt lifecycle. From Gemini 2.5 model hardening, to purpose-built machine learning (ML) models detecting malicious instructions, to system-level safeguards, we are meaningfully elevating the difficulty, expense, and complexity faced by an attacker. This approach compels adversaries to resort to methods that are either more easily identified or demand greater resources. Our model training with adversarial data significantly enhanced our defenses against indirect prompt

enrichment.technical_details: Indirect prompt injection: an attacker embeds hidden malicious instructions inside external content (e.g., emails, documents, calendar invites, web pages) that a generative-AI assistant (Gemini) later retrieves and processes as part of its context. Unlike direct prompt injection, where the user supplies a malicious prompt, the malicious payload rides in on supposedly trusted data, instructing the model to exfiltrate user data, perform unauthorized actions, or otherwise deviate from the user's intent. The post notes Gemini defenses are trained on real-world examples.
enrichment.affected_products: Gemini 2.5, Gemini app, Gemini in Google Workspace (Gmail, Docs editors, Drive, Chat)
enrichment.exploit_available: No public proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit targeting Google Gemini is referenced in this June 2025 advisory. The post cites background academic research (Ben Nassi 'Confidentiality'; Stav Cohen, Technion; Or Yair, SafeBreach) and references EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711) as an industry example — but EchoLeak applies to Microsoft 365 Copilot and is explicitly noted as not applicable to Gemini. A later SafeBreach Labs disclosure (June 2026) demonstrated a Gemini prompt-injection exploit via WhatsApp — see https://www.safebreach.com/blog/gemini-voice-assistant-prompt-injection-exploit/ — but is outside this blog post's scope.
enrichment.patch_available: No traditional software patch version is published. Google states it is rolling out enhanced indirect prompt-injection defenses (proprietary ML content classifiers newly deployed) for Gemini 2.5, the Gemini app, and Gemini in Google Workspace, and committed to introducing additional prompt-injection defenses later in 2025. References: https://blog.google/security/mitigating-prompt-injection-attacks/ and https://knowledge.workspace.google.com/admin/security/indirect-prompt-injections-and-googles-layered-defense-strategy-for-gemini
enrichment.active_exploitation: The June 2025 Google Security Blog post does not report a specific in-the-wild exploitation campaign against Gemini. It characterizes indirect prompt injection as an emerging attack vector, uses 'real-world examples' to train defenses, and references EchoLeak (CVE-2025-32711) — a zero-click image-rendering exfiltration in Microsoft 365 Copilot — as a related industry example, explicitly noting EchoLeak was not applicable to Gemini. A later Google follow-up (April 2026, 'AI threats in the wild') observed indirect-prompt-injection attempts on the public web and described attacker sophistication as 'still low'; no named campaign targeting Gemini. Reporting sources: https://blog.google/security/prompt-injections-web/ and https://www.securityweek.com/malicious-ai-prompt-injection-attacks-increasing-but-sophistication-still-low-google/
enrichment.mitigation: Google deploys a layered (defense-in-depth) strategy for Gemini 2.5, the Gemini app, and Gemini in Google Workspace, comprising: (1) model hardening via adversarial training; (2) proprietary ML-based prompt-injection content classifiers that detect and filter malicious instructions in inbound emails and attachments; (3) 'security thought reinforcement' — targeted system instructions that nudge the model to prioritize user intent over adversarial instructions; (4) markdown sanitization combined with suspicious-URL redaction using Google Safe Browsing (blocking external image URLs to prevent rendering-based exfiltration such as EchoLeak); (5) a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) user-confirmation framework requiring explicit user approval for sensitive operations (e.g., deleting calendar events); and (6) end-user security notifications when Gemini blocks or suspects an attack, with 'Learn more' links.

findings.title: Countering Chinese State-Sponsored Actors Compromise of Networks Worldwide to Feed Global Espionage System
findings.summary: Executive summary People’s Republic of China (PRC) state-sponsored cyber threat actors are targeting networks globally, including, but not limited to, telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks. While these actors focus on large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers, as well as provider edge (PE) and customer edge (CE) routers, they also leverage compromised devices and trusted connections to pivot into other networks. These actors often modify routers to maintain persistent, long-term access to networks. This activity partially overlaps with cyber threat actor reporting by the cybersecurity industry—commonly referred to as Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor, among others. The authoring agencies are not adopting a particular commercial naming convention and hereafter refer to those responsible for the cyber threat activity more generically as “Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) actors” throughout this advisory. This cluster of cyber threat activity has been observed in the United States, Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the United Kingdom, and other areas globally. This Cybersecurity Advisory (CSA) includes observations from various government and industry investigations where the APT actors targeted internal enterprise environments, as well as systems and networks that deliver services directly to customers. This CSA details the tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) leveraged by these APT actors to facilitate detection and threat hunting, and provides mitigation guidance to reduce the risk from these APT actors and their TTPs. This CSA is being released by the following authoring and co-sealing agencies: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United States Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3) Australian Signals Directorate’s Austra

enrichment.technical_details: PRC state-sponsored actors target Internet-exposed network edge devices (large backbone/PE/CE routers, Ivanti Connect Secure / PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN gateways, Cisco IOS XE/IOS XR/Smart Install, etc.) using publicly known N-day CVEs (command injection, authentication bypass, arbitrary file creation, command injection in web components like CVE-2024-21887/CVE-2023-46805/Ivanti, CVE-2024-3400/Palo Alto, CVE-2023-20198/CVE-2023-20273/Cisco IOS XE, CVE-2018-0171/Smart Install) as initial access. After gaining a foothold, actors pivot via compromised devices and trusted provider-to-provider/provider-to-customer interconnections. Persistence is established by modifying Access Control Lists (e.g., recurring 'access-list 20', '10', or '50' entries) to whitelist attacker IPs, opening high TCP ports for SSH/SFTP/HTTP(S) on non-default ports, installing attacker SSH keys, enabling Cisco Guest Shell containers on IOS XE / NX-OS, modifying TACACS+/RADIUS AAA configurations, and enabling the IOS XR sshd_operns service on TCP/57722 on the network device to get direct shell access with sudo. GRE, mGRE, and IPsec tunnels are used for C2 and exfiltration; packet capture is performed (often within a Guest Shell container) to harvest credentials; router configs, BGP routes, and subscriber records are enumerated; local logs are cleared and log forwarding is disabled for evasion; data is exfiltrated back to China.
enrichment.affected_products: Ivanti Connect Secure and Ivanti Policy Secure (CVE-2024-21887, commonly chained with CVE-2023-46805); Palo Alto Networks PAN-OS GlobalProtect feature (CVE-2024-3400); Cisco IOS XE Web Management User Interface (CVE-2023-20273 and CVE-2023-20198); Cisco Smart Install (CVE-2018-0171); Cisco IOS XR (sshd_operns service on TCP/57722); broader edge-device categories including large backbone routers of major telecommunications providers and provider-edge (PE)/customer-edge (CE) routers; related reporting also references Fortinet, Juniper, and Microsoft Exchange edge devices.
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes. All CVEs cited are well-known N-days with widely published PoC and weaponized exploit code, including public Ivanti Connect Secure RCE exploits for CVE-2024-21887/CVE-2023-46805, public PAN-OS GlobalProtect command-injection PoCs for CVE-2024-3400, and public Cisco IOS XE web UI authentication-bypass / post-auth RCE exploits for CVE-2023-20198 and CVE-2023-20273.
enrichment.patch_available: Yes. Vendor patches are publicly available for all CVEs cited in AA25-239A. Prioritized patching of CVE-2024-21887 (Ivanti Connect Secure / Policy Secure), CVE-2024-3400 (Palo Alto PAN-OS GlobalProtect), CVE-2023-20198 and CVE-2023-20273 (Cisco IOS XE), CVE-2023-46805 (Ivanti authentication bypass), and CVE-2018-0171 (Cisco Smart Install) is expressly recommended in the advisory. Consolidated patch guidance is at: https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-239a
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes - widespread, confirmed in-the-wild exploitation. The advisory documents ongoing PRC state-sponsored campaigns that have already compromised telecommunications providers and other critical-infrastructure organizations globally (including telecommunications, government, transportation, lodging, and military infrastructure networks). The actors maintain long-term persistence by modifying router configurations and establishing redundant backdoors. Reporting sources: CISA AA25-239A (https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-239a); NSA joint press release (https://www.nsa.gov/Press-Room/Press-Releases-Statements/Press-Release-View/Article/4287371/nsa-and-others-provide-guidance-to-counter-china-state-sponsored-actors-targeti/); BSI Salt-Typhoon CSA reprint (https://www.bsi.bund.de/SharedDocs/Downloads/EN/BSI/Cyber-Security/CSA_Salt-Typhoon.pdf).
enrichment.threat_actors: PRC state-sponsored threat actor clusters: Salt Typhoon, OPERATOR PANDA, RedMike, UNC5807, and GhostEmperor. NSA-coordinated reporting further attributes the activity to affiliated entities Sichuan Juxinhe Network Technology Co. Ltd., Beijing Huanyu Tianqiong Information Technology Co. Ltd., and Sichuan Zhixin Ruijie Network Technology Co. Ltd.
enrichment.mitigation: Per CISA/NSA/FBI guidance: (1) Prioritize patching CVE-2024-21887, CVE-2024-3400, CVE-2023-20198, CVE-2023-20273, CVE-2023-46805, and CVE-2018-0171 across all Internet-exposed edge devices; (2) Regularly pull running device configurations, compare against authorized baselines, and audit for unauthorized GRE/IPsec tunnels, unexpected packet capture/SPAN/RSPAN, changes to TACACS+/RADIUS servers, and unusual ACLs; (3) Enforce a robust change-management process and periodic configuration auditing; (4) Identify full scope of compromise before attempting mitigation/eviction; (5) Disable outbound connections from management/VTY interfaces (e.g., 'transport output none'); (6) Disable unused ports/protocols and require encrypted, authenticated management (SSH, SFTP/SCP, HTTPS) with SNMPv3; (7) Change default administrative credentials and require public-key authentication; (8) Use strong cryptography: Diffie-Hellman Group 20 (384-bit ECP), AES-256, SHA-384; (9) Cisco-specific hardening: disable Cisco Smart Install; protect local credentials with Type 8 (PBKDF2-SHA-256) or Type 6 (AES); audit for unauthorized sshd_operns on TCP/57722; disable web config UI ('no ip http server', 'no ip http secure-server'); add 'deny any any log' terminator to all ACLs; disable Guest Shell ('guestshell disable', 'guestshell destroy') and IOx ('no iox'); (10) Validate firmware/image hashes against vendor-provided values and enable signed-image enforcement; (11) Maintain enhanced visibility/hardening guidance for communications infrastructure.

findings.title: Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies
findings.summary: Executive Summary This joint cybersecurity advisory (CSA) highlights a Russian state-sponsored cyber campaign targeting Western logistics entities and technology companies. This includes those involved in the coordination, transport, and delivery of foreign assistance to Ukraine. Since 2022, Western logistics entities and IT companies have faced an elevated risk of targeting by the Russian General Staff Main Intelligence Directorate (GRU) 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165—tracked in the cybersecurity community under several names (see “ Cybersecurity Industry Tracking ”). The actors’ cyber espionage-oriented campaign, targeting technology companies and logistics entities, uses a mix of previously disclosed tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs). The authoring agencies expect similar targeting and TTP use to continue. Executives and network defenders at logistics entities and technology companies should recognize the elevated threat of unit 26165 targeting, increase monitoring and threat hunting for known TTPs and indicators of compromise (IOCs), and posture network defenses with a presumption of targeting. This cyber espionage-oriented campaign targeting logistics entities and technology companies uses a mix of previously disclosed TTPs and is likely connected to these actors’ wide scale targeting of IP cameras in Ukraine and bordering NATO nations. The following authors and co-sealers are releasing this CSA: United States National Security Agency (NSA) United States Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) United Kingdom National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC-UK) Germany Federal Intelligence Service (BND) Bundesnachrichtendienst Germany Federal Office for Information Security (BSI) Bundesamt für Sicherheit in der Informationstechnik Germany Federal Office for the Protection of the Constitution (BfV) Bundesamt für Verfassungsschutz Czech Republic Military Intelligence (VZ) Vojenské zpravodajství Czech Republic National Cyber and Infor

enrichment.technical_details: GRU Unit 26165 conducted an espionage campaign against Western logistics/transport/IT companies supporting Ukraine aid shipments, stealing shipment schedules, manifests, routes, and donor information. Initial-access TTPs include spearphishing attachments/links (T1566.001, T1566.002), voice phishing (T1566.004), password spraying (T1110.003) via Tor/VPN anonymization, and exploitation of public-facing applications. Key exploited flaws: (1) CVE-2023-23397 in Outlook for Windows — a crafted calendar/email message triggers a connection to an attacker-controlled SMB/UNC endpoint, leaking the victim's Net-NTLMv2 hash for relay/authentication; (2) CVE-2023-38831 in WinRAR — code execution when the user views a benign file (e.g., .jpg) inside a ZIP archive with a specially crafted inner filename; (3) Roundcube Webmail XSS/SQL-injection flaws (CVE-2020-35730, CVE-2021-44026, CVE-2020-12641) for command execution and credential theft. Post-compromise TTPs include mailbox-permission manipulation and silent MFA enrollment (T1098.002, T1556.006), lateral movement via Impacket/PsExec/RDP (T1021.001), AD dumping via ntdsutil.exe and Volume Shadow Copies (T1003.003, T1552.006), credential theft via Certipy/ADExplorer/ldap-dump.py/Get-GPPPassword.py, custom malware HEADLACE/MASEPIE/OCEANMAP/STEELHOOK, and exfiltration via OpenSSH/TLS-encrypted/.zip archives. The actor also attacked IP cameras (primarily in Ukraine) with brute-forced credentials and RTSP requests to capture border-area imagery.
enrichment.affected_products: RARLAB WinRAR before 6.23 (CVE-2023-38831); Microsoft Outlook for Windows (CVE-2023-23397); Roundcube Webmail before 1.3.17 and 1.4.x before 1.4.12 (CVE-2021-44026); Roundcube Webmail before 1.2.13, 1.3.x before 1.3.16, and 1.4.x before 1.4.x (CVE-2020-35730); Roundcube Webmail before 1.4.4 (CVE-2020-12641); IP cameras / IP-based border-surveillance devices reachable via RTSP (mostly in Ukraine); SOHO routers and corporate VPN appliances abused as anonymization infrastructure.
enrichment.cvss_vector: CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H (for the most severe CVE referenced in the advisory, CVE-2023-23397 in Microsoft Outlook; CVE-2023-38831 WinRAR carries CVSS:3.1/AV:L/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H at 7.8 HIGH; CVE-2021-44026 Roundcube at 9.8 CRITICAL; CVE-2020-35730 Roundcube XSS at 6.1 MEDIUM).
enrichment.exploit_available: Yes — public proof-of-concept and weaponized exploits are available. CVE-2023-23397 (Microsoft Outlook) was first weaponized by APT28 as a zero-day starting in 2022 before Microsoft's March 2023 patch; a public PoC is hosted at https://github.com/Trackflaw/CVE-2023-23397 (exfiltrates the victim's NetNTLMv2 digest via SMB). CVE-2023-38831 (WinRAR) was exploited as a zero-day in spearphishing campaigns and is listed in CISA's KEV catalog. The Roundcube CVEs (CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2020-35730, CVE-2021-44026) have been weaponized by APT28 in Roundcube-spearphishing campaigns with detection signatures published (e.g., SOC Prime).
enrichment.patch_available: Yes — vendor patches are available for all listed products. Microsoft Outlook (CVE-2023-23397): security update released 14 March 2023 — https://msrc.microsoft.com/update-guide/vulnerability/cve-2023-23397. RARLAB WinRAR (CVE-2023-38831): WinRAR version 6.23 released 2 August 2023 — https://www.win-rar.com/singlenewsview.html?&L=0&tx_ttnews%5Btt_news%5D=232. Roundcube Webmail (CVE-2021-44026): upgrade to 1.4.12 or later (and 1.3.17 or later for 1.3.x). Roundcube Webmail (CVE-2020-35730): upgrade to 1.2.13 / 1.3.16 / 1.4.x or later. CISA advisory with downloadable IOC bundle (AA25-141A STIX): https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a.
enrichment.active_exploitation: Yes — confirmed active in-the-wild exploitation by GRU 85th GTsSS Unit 26165 since 2022, with ongoing activity documented through at least 2025. The CISA advisory co-sealed by FBI, NSA, Cyber Command, ASD (Australia), and NCSC (UK) states the actor 'almost certainly uses more infrastructure and TTPs than are listed.' CVE-2023-23397 is listed in CISA's Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog and was used as a zero-day by APT28 prior to the March 2023 patch. AttackIQ released an assessment template emulating Unit 26165's TTPs aligned with AA25-141A on 21 May 2025; SafeBreach and SOC Prime published detection/emulation coverage for the same IOCs.
enrichment.threat_actors: Russian GRU 85th Main Special Service Center (85th GTsSS), military unit 26165 — tracked as APT28, Fancy Bear, Forest Blizzard, BlueDelta, Pawn Storm, Sofacy Group, Sednit, and STRONTIUM. The advisory documents a long-running cyber-espionage campaign since 2022 targeting Western logistics entities, IT companies, and technology exporters involved in coordinating aid to Ukraine.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply the Microsoft Outlook security update from 14 March 2023 to remediate CVE-2023-23397; if patching is delayed, Microsoft recommends disabling the Windows WebClient (WebDAV) service or blocking outbound SMB/TCP 445/139 from user endpoints. Upgrade WinRAR to version 6.23 or newer (released 2 August 2023) for CVE-2023-38831. Apply Roundcube Webmail vendor patches (upgrade to releases fixing CVE-2020-35730, CVE-2020-12641, CVE-2021-44026). Implement network segmentation, Zero Trust principles, and host firewalls to block lateral movement. Deploy EDR on mail servers and domain controllers; audit access logs and monitor unexpected Windows-event-log clearing (T1070.001). Block external NTLM/SMB requests, review remote-access authentication, audit IP-camera/theft-of-service accounts. Enable Microsoft Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules to prevent executable content launched from email and globally writable directories such as %APPDATA%. Harden IP cameras by disabling UPnP, P2P, and 'anonymous visit' features, disabling unused ports (FTP, web), and enabling authenticated RTSP only.

findings.title: Browser Security: Zero-Days Are Only Part of the Problem

enrichment.technical_details: The article does not describe a single vulnerability; instead it surveys the browser attack surface broadly. Technical mechanisms named include exploitation of rendering logic and JavaScript execution, document-handling flaws, memory-safety weaknesses enabling sandbox escape and privilege escalation, clickjacking, cross-site scripting, HTML smuggling, adversary-in-the-middle (AiTM) phishing capable of bypassing MFA, session/cookie abuse (including token replay across geographies), malicious or over-privileged browser extensions, and web-based social engineering against trusted SaaS login pages. The core thesis is that zero-day browser memory-corruption bugs represent a small fraction of real browser risk, while identity- and session-based intrusions using these techniques dominate attacker activity.
enrichment.affected_products: Chromium-based web browsers (no specific product versions cited)
enrichment.exploit_available: No specific proof-of-concept or weaponized exploit is associated with this CrowdStrike blog post. The article is a general awareness/thought-leadership piece discussing categories of browser attack techniques (e.g., rendering/JavaScript/memory-safety flaws, sandbox escapes, phishing/AiTM credential theft, session hijacking, HTML smuggling, malicious extensions) rather than a single exploitable vulnerability. The blog does not link to any specific PoC repository, GitHub project, or weaponized exploit code.
enrichment.patch_available: No specific vendor patch is associated with this advisory (it is a general-awareness blog, not a CVE write-up). General hardening guidance is to ensure Chromium-based browsers are kept up to date with the latest security releases and to enable automatic updates. CrowdStrike product reference: https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/resources/data-sheets/falcon-secure-access/
enrichment.active_exploitation: The article does not attribute exploitation to any specific CVE. It cites general industry statistics arguing that zero-day and pre-disclosure exploitation of browsers is widespread: (1) the Verizon 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report states that vulnerability exploitation surpassed stolen credentials as the top breach entry point in 2025 (https://www.verizon.com/about/news/breach-industry-wide-dbir-finds); and (2) the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report found that 42% of vulnerabilities were exploited before public disclosure (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/global-threat-report/). No specific in-the-wild campaign, APT group, or ransomware operator is named in the post.
enrichment.mitigation: Apply browser security patches promptly and keep Chromium-based browsers (Chrome, Microsoft Edge) on the latest auto-updated releases to address rendering and memory-safety flaws. Layer in identity-centric defenses against AiTM phishing, session token theft, MFA bypass, and malicious/over-privileged browser extensions. CrowdStrike cites its Falcon Secure Access product, which uses JavaScript Language Randomization (JSLR) to randomize the browser's JavaScript runtime, as a recommended defense-in-depth control. Supporting reports referenced in the post include the CrowdStrike 2026 Global Threat Report (https://www.crowdstrike.com/en-us/global-threat-report/) and the Verizon 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report (https://www.verizon.com/about/news/breach-industry-wide-dbir-finds).

findings.title: 94% of Organizations Report Cloud Breaches: CrowdStrike State of CDR Survey
In Other News: Canadian Hacker Jailed, Open Source Zero-Days, Two Sentenced for ATM Jackpotting
findings.summary: Noteworthy stories that might have slipped under the radar: Anonymous-linked Canadian hacker jailed, researcher drops zero-days in open source projects, Venezuelans sentenced in the US over ATM jackpotting. The post In Other News: Canadian Hacker Jailed, Open Source Zero-Days, Two Sentenced for ATM Jackpotting appeared first on SecurityWeek .

findings.title: Bringing Rust to the Pixel Baseband
findings.summary: Posted by Jiacheng Lu, Software Engineer, Google Pixel Team Google is continuously advancing the security of Pixel devices. We have been focusing on hardening the cellular baseband modem against exploitation. Recognizing the risks associated within the complex modem firmware, Pixel 9 shipped with mitigations against a range of memory-safety vulnerabilities. For Pixel 10, Google is advancing its proactive security measures further. Following our previous discussion on "Deploying Rust in Existing Firmware Codebases" , this post shares a concrete application: integrating a memory-safe Rust DNS(Domain Name System) parser into the modem firmware. The new Rust-based DNS parser significantly reduces our security risk by mitigating an entire class of vulnerabilities in a risky area, while also laying the foundation for broader adoption of memory-safe code in other areas. Here we share our experience of working on it, and hope it can inspire the use of more memory safe languages in low-level environments. Why Modem Memory Safety Can’t Wait In recent years, we have seen increasing interest in the cellular modem from attackers and security researchers. For example, Google's Project Zero gained remote code execution on Pixel modems over the Internet. Pixel modem has tens of Megabytes of executable code. Given the complexity and remote attack surface of the modem, other critical memory safety vulnerabilities may remain in the predominantly memory-unsafe firmware code. Why DNS? The DNS protocol is most commonly known in the context of browsers finding websites. With the evolution of cellular technology, modern cellular communications have migrated to digital data networks; consequently, even basic operations such as call forwarding rely on DNS services. DNS is a complex protocol and requires parsing of untrusted data, which can lead to vulnerabilities, particularly when implemented in a memory-unsafe language (example: CVE-2024-27227 ). Implementing the DNS parser in Rust offers
